drills.title: The Departure Counted Twice
drills.prompt: Kelmorrow's revenue from its regular customer base fell 12.8% over the year. The board asks: **what makes up the decline, and what should we act on?**
drills.anchor_metric: decline in revenue from the base = 12.8%

exhibit.title: Cut A — by type of change

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Competitors opened nearby"** | the base is falling → "someone took our shopper" | The branch calls for a map of competitor openings. The exhibit shows the main group **did not leave for a competitor but moved to the chain's own online channel** — the shopper stayed, the way they buy changed. The branch's data does not distinguish the two cases |
| **"Prices went up"** | the second reflex | Average basket in the exhibit **does not fall** for the groups that reduced frequency; the branch is refuted |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Outright customer departure** | "churn" · "left altogether" · "customer loss" | Without it you cannot separate losing a customer from a change in their behaviour. These are different diseases: recovering a lost customer and recovering lost frequency need different treatments |
| **2. Behaviour change among those who stayed** | "frequency and basket" · "how purchases changed" · "behaviour of the retained base" | Without it the decline is explained by churn alone, and "retain customers" is unsupported if the customers never left |
| **3. Migration between the chain's own channels** | "migration to online" · "movement between our channels" · "channel shift" | **Load-bearing and non-obvious.** A shopper who moved from our offline store to our own online channel is **not lost** — but in offline reporting they look like churn. "We are losing the base" is unsupported without this branch |
key.me:
> **This slot trains absorption BETWEEN levels.** A pairwise matrix of the first level **cannot detect it by construction** — at the first level there is no overlap. The matrix must therefore include "second-level sub-branch ↔ first-level branch" pairs. Without that, the body would violate the very norm it trains.

**Level 1 × Level 1:**

| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — those who left and those who stayed do not intersect by definition |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** at the first level |
| 2 ↔ 3 | **independent** at the first level |

**Level 2 × Level 1 — this is where absorption appears:**

| Sub-branch (L2) | Parent | Overlaps with (L1) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Left for online" | branch 1 (churn) | **branch 3** (channel migration) | ⚠️ **ABSORPTION** — this is the same set of shoppers |
| "Reduced frequency because of online" | branch 2 (behaviour) | **branch 3** | ⚠️ **ABSORPTION** — the same partially migrated group |
| "Left for a competitor" | branch 1 | — | independent |
| "Reduced basket" | branch 2 | — | independent |

**Resolution rule:** the group "partially moved to online" must be assigned to **exactly one** branch. A tree that places it in both churn and channel migration counts it **twice**.
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Outright departure | share of the base with no purchase at all · their former share of revenue |
| 2. Behaviour of those who stayed | purchase frequency · average basket · trend by cohort |
| 3. Channel migration | share of the base buying online · their combined spend across both channels |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **speed of verification** | branch 3 | matching customer IDs across channels is one query |
| size of effect | branch 1 | outright departure is the largest loss per shopper |
| cost of data | branch 2 | till data is already held |
key.s0:
The candidate builds "churn / behaviour of those who stayed / channel migration." **At the first level this is faultless MECE** — those who left, those who stayed, and those who switched channel do not overlap as categories.

The overlap appears **at the second level**: the sub-branch "left for online" inside churn is the same group as the whole of branch 3. A pairwise matrix of the first level does not reveal it.

**Top echelon: YES.** The tree passes a standard MECE check and still counts 11.2% of the base twice.

**Separation from ST-003 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-003 the absorption sits **within one level** — "fuel" stands beside "variable costs," and a pairwise check of the first level catches it. Here there is **no first-level overlap at all**; it exists only between levels. Different operations, different means of detection.

drills.title: Profitable Customer, Loss-Making Order
drills.prompt: Thornquay's gross margin is 16.1%; the board's target is 19%. The board asks: **where do we find the missing 2.9 pp?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Drop the loss-making customers"** | the standard move when working on margin | Per the exhibit **all three customer types are profitable** (15.7–16.1%). The branch calls for a customer margin ranking and will not find a single loss-maker — the data exists and no conclusion follows from it |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Price and discounts** | "pricing" · "discount depth" · "realised price" | One of the two components of margin; without it the gap is unsupported |
| **2. Cost to serve** | "servicing costs" · "cost of fulfilment" · "cost-to-serve" | The second component; without it price pressure cannot be separated from cost growth |
| **3. Unit of analysis: what we measure margin on** | "in which cut we look" · "customer or order" · "level of measurement" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** Margin per **customer** and margin per **order** give different answers on the same revenue. "There are no loss-makers" is unsupported if you look only at the customer |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — the components of margin |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** — price vs the unit of measurement |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — cost to serve depends on the unit (per order vs per customer). Separated: branch 2 is the **magnitude** of cost; branch 3 is the **base** it is charged to |
| Loss-making customers ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **special case** — the branch is an application of the "customer" unit; parallel placement earns a warning |
| Loss-making customers ↔ 1, 2 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Price | realised price by order type · discount depth |
| 2. Cost to serve | cost of processing an order · share of fixed cost per order |
| 3. Unit of analysis | margin **per customer** and margin **per order** on the same data · number of orders with negative margin |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **fit to the question** | branch 3 | until the unit is chosen, the other branches are computed on the wrong base |
| size of effect | branch 2 | cost to serve usually exceeds the price effect in distribution |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | price lists are at hand |
key.s0:
The tree "price / cost / customers" is correct as a type — it is the right profit tree for a distributor. The unit of analysis chosen is the **customer**, and in that unit there are no loss-makers: 15.7%, 16.0%, 16.1%.

The right unit for a margin question is the **order**: 4 280 orders (37.6% of all) run at a margin of **−3.5%**, and they are spread across every customer type, including the large and profitable ones.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who asks "in which cut are we measuring margin" reaches the right answer. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-004 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-004 the **type** of tree is wrong — a profit tree for a foundation with no revenue; the tree is not about that organisation at all. Here the tree type is **right**: a profit tree suits a distributor. What is wrong is the **unit of analysis** inside a right type. The ST-004 candidate builds the wrong tree; the ST-022 candidate builds the right tree on the wrong base.

drills.title: Elegant and Unmeasurable
drills.prompt: Maldravin is losing tenders: it wins 18% against 31% a year ago. **What would you check to find the cause?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Satisfaction among competitors' customers"** | it sounds like a proper benchmarking metric | The metric is **named and unmeasurable**: the company has no access, and cannot get access, to surveys of competitors' customers. The branch looks substantive and produces not a single figure |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Our bid: price and terms** | "what we offer" · "price and lead time" · "bid parameters" | Without it you cannot link the losses to anything we do. The cause is unsupported |
| **2. Tender requirements and our compliance** | "selection criteria" · "buyer requirements" · "do we qualify formally" | Without it you cannot separate losing on price from being excluded on requirements |
| **3. Who we are losing to** | "the winners" · "competitors in tenders" · "who wins" | Without an addressee you cannot see what actually outweighs us: price, timing or compliance |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — our offer vs the requirements |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — the winner complies with the requirements better. Separated: branch 2 is **our** compliance; branch 3 is **their** advantage |
| Competitor satisfaction ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **absorption** — a property of the winner sits inside branch 3 |
| Competitor satisfaction ↔ 1, 2 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure | Available to us |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Our bid | our price against the winner's · quoted lead times | yes — tender records are published |
| 2. Requirements | share of tenders where we failed formally · which requirements we miss | yes — rejections arrive with reasons |
| 3. Winners | who won · their price and lead time | yes — from the records |
| ~~Satisfaction among competitors' customers~~ | ~~NPS of a competitor's customers~~ | **no — the data does not exist in any accessible form** |

**Slot norm:** a metric counts only if its **source** is named. A metric without a source does not score.
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| speed of verification | branch 2 | rejections with reasons arrive automatically |
| size of effect | branch 1 | price usually decides a tender |
| cost of data | branch 3 | tender records are free and public |
key.s0:
This slot differs from ST-006 in that the metric **is named**. "Satisfaction among competitors' customers" sounds analytically mature — and it is unworkable, because the data does not exist in any form the company can reach.

The muscle: state not only **what** to measure but **where it comes from**.

**Top echelon: NO** — by construction. An honest **Easy**.

**Separation from ST-006 ([DUP], substantive):** ST-006 — a branch with **no metric at all**, the label "Competitors" with nothing inside. Here the metric **exists, is specific, and is unmeasurable**. In ST-006 the candidate did not think it through; in ST-023 the candidate thought it through to the end and ran into an absence of data without noticing.

drills.title: Arrived at the Wrong Place
drills.prompt: Ostrevane's operating profit fell by $14.2M over the year. **Build the tree and say in what order you will work through it.**

exhibit.title: Ostrevane: what made up the operating profit decline, $M

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Staff costs"** | the largest line in transport, worked through first by habit | The branch is **relevant**, but per the exhibit it accounts for **$1.1M of $14.2M**. Worked first and to exhaustion, it consumes the time before the candidate reaches fuel and load factor |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Revenue: volume and fare** | "income" · "passengers and price" · "revenue" | Without it you cannot separate falling demand from rising costs |
| **2. Variable costs** | "fuel and maintenance" · "cost per trip" · "variable" | The largest contributor per the exhibit; without it the conclusion is unsupported |
| **3. Load factor and route structure** | "seat occupancy" · "schedule efficiency" · "route mix" | Without it, falling revenue and rising cost per passenger are indistinguishable |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — income vs costs |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **double-coverage risk** — load factor affects revenue. Separated: branch 1 is **fare and passenger count**; branch 3 is the **ratio** of passengers to seats |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — cost per trip is divided across passengers. Separated the same way |
| Staff ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **absorption** — staff sits inside costs; parallel placement breaks ME |
| Staff ↔ 1, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Revenue | passengers · average fare · revenue per trip |
| 2. Variable costs | cost per trip by line · fuel price trend |
| 3. Load factor | seat occupancy · trips scheduled · cost per passenger |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **size of effect** | branch 2 | fuel accounts for $8.9M of $14.2M = **62.7%** |
| speed of verification | branch 3 | load factor is visible in the booking system at once |
| cost of data | branch 1 | sales data is internal |

**The defect in this slot is not in the structure of the tree but in the order:** the tree may be complete and correct, the metrics correctly assigned, and yet the analysis begins with staff ($1.1M) and never reaches fuel ($8.9M).
key.s0:
The tree is complete, the metrics are present, and depth is **allocated correctly** — each branch is worked in proportion. The error is in the **order**: the candidate begins with staff, because that is the familiar line, and within the time available never reaches fuel.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who checks the order against the size of contribution reaches the right answer. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-013 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-013 the depth went **into the wrong branch** — three levels into a line with no contribution while the load-bearing one stayed a label. Here depth is allocated **correctly**; what is wrong is the **sequence**: the same map walked in the wrong order. ST-013 is a defect of the tree; ST-024 is a defect of the route through it.

drills.title: Named, but in the Wrong Role
drills.prompt: Calveska has launched a generic priced 22% below the originator. After eight months its market share is 1.4% against a plan of 12%. The board asks: **why are they not taking it, and what should we do?**

exhibit.title: Calveska: the chain from prescription to purchase
rows: ask for the brand by name: 9%
**determines the choice at the point of purchase**
product on the list: **2 chains of 11**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Doctors don't know about the product"** | low share → "we need an awareness campaign" | The exhibit shows: **awareness among doctors is 84%**, and 61% prescribe it. The branch calls for data on medical reps, from which the gap between prescription and purchase does not follow |
| **"The price isn't low enough"** | the classic for generics | The price is **22% below** the originator and below two competing generics. The branch is refuted by the prompt |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The product and its substitutability** | "equivalence" · "attributes" · "can it be substituted" | Without it you cannot assert the problem lies outside the product |
| **2. Demand: doctors and patients** | "are they prescribing" · "are they asking for it" · "prescriber behaviour" | Without it you cannot separate absent demand from demand that cannot be met |
| **3. Every participant in the chain **and their roles** | "stakeholders" · "who is in the chain" · "who decides what" · "role of each" | **Load-bearing.** The pharmacy chain appears in most candidates' trees — as a **sales channel**. In fact it **determines the choice at the point of purchase**: with equivalent generics, the pharmacist offers whichever is on the chain's list. "Work harder with doctors" is unsupported if the decision is not made there |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — attributes vs demand |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **role-conflation risk** — the doctor creates demand, the pharmacy converts it. Separated: branch 2 is the **creation** of demand; branch 3 is its **execution** at the point of purchase. A candidate who files the pharmacy under branch 2 as a "channel" loses its decisive role |
| Awareness ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **absorption** |
| Price ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial |
| Awareness ↔ 1, 3 | **independent** |
| Price ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| Awareness ↔ Price | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Product | bioequivalence · dosage form against competitors |
| 2. Demand | % of doctors aware · % prescribing · number of prescriptions |
| 3. Participants and roles | **the role of each**: creates demand / executes / blocks · presence on chain lists · share of chains stocking the product |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **size of effect** | branch 3 | absence from the list blocks the sale regardless of demand |
| speed of verification | branch 3 | list presence is one query to the chains |
| cost of data | branch 2 | medical rep data is internal |
key.s0:
The pharmacy chain **is named** in nearly every candidate's tree — under "sales channels," as a link in distribution. The tree looks complete: product, doctors, patients, channels, regulator.

The error is in the **role**: the chain is filed under execution when in fact it **makes the choice**, with 87% of prescriptions written under INN and only 9% of patients asking for a brand. The product is on the list of **2 chains of 11**.

**Top echelon: YES.** The actor is present in the tree, the tree is complete, and the conclusion is wrong because the actor has been assigned the wrong function.

**Separation from ST-014 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-014 the insurance payer is **absent** from the tree — the row reads "not surveyed," the actor is never named. Here the pharmacy chain **is named and sits in the tree**, but as a channel rather than as the decision-maker. ST-014 is an omission; ST-025 is a **misattribution of role**. Detection differs too: there you look for a missing row, here for a mismatch between two columns in an existing one.

drills.title: The Goal Beneath the Goal
drills.prompt: Two years ago Pyrenvold launched a retention programme. Churn has fallen from 24% to 13% — the board's target is met. The board asks: **do we continue the programme as it stands?**

exhibit.title: Pyrenvold: economics of the retention programme

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Benchmark churn against competitors"** | the churn target is met → "how do others do it" | The branch calls for industry churn benchmarks. Even complete, they confirm or refute only **the achievement of an intermediate goal**, which is already achieved. Whether it was worth achieving **does not follow from them** |
| **"Satisfaction among retained customers"** | a retention programme → NPS | Loyalty to a discount is not customer profitability; the branch produces no cash result |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Achievement of the stated goal** | "is the KPI met" · "churn trend" · "result on the metric" | Without it you cannot assert the programme works as intended. This is a necessary part of the answer |
| **2. The cost of achieving it** | "what it cost" · "programme costs" · "cost of retention" | Without it, meeting the goal does not translate into money. "Continue, churn is down" is unsupported |
| **3. The goal one level up, and its movement** | "why we cut churn" · "the ultimate goal" · "what sits behind this goal" · "hierarchy of goals" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** Churn is reduced for the sake of **profit**. If the customers retained cost more to keep than they bring, the intermediate goal is met **against** the ultimate one. A conclusion about the programme is unsupported without checking the goal above it |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — the result vs its cost |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **level-conflation risk** — both concern "the goal." Separated: branch 1 is the **stated** goal (churn); branch 3 is the goal **for whose sake** it was set (profit). A candidate who merges them loses the hierarchy and reads achieving churn as achieving the goal |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — programme costs enter profit. Separated: branch 2 is the **direct** cost of the programme; branch 3 is the **full** result on retained customers |
| Benchmark ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **absorption** — a comparison is an assessment of the same metric |
| Benchmark ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| NPS ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial |
| NPS ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| Benchmark ↔ NPS | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Stated goal | churn by year · number of customers retained |
| 2. Cost of achievement | direct programme costs · depth of discounts granted |
| 3. Goal one level up | **profit from retained customers** by year · revenue less cost of retention less cost to serve |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **hierarchy of goals** | branch 3 | until the ultimate goal is checked, meeting the intermediate one settles nothing |
| size of effect | branch 2 | cost of retention is usually the largest component |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | churn is calculated instantly |
key.s0:
The board's goal is stated explicitly and has been met: churn 24% → 13%. The candidate builds a correct tree around churn — trend, programme cost, satisfaction — and everything reconciles.

The error is that **reducing churn is not the ultimate goal**. It was set for the sake of profit, and retention runs through a growing personal discount: revenue from retained customers +34.1, but the cost of retaining them −27.6 and cost to serve −11.9. Over three years the programme delivers **−$10.5M**.

**Top echelon: YES.** The stated goal is met, the tree around it is correct, and the answer "continue" is wrong.

**The sign flip is honest.** There is no discounting at all and no rate exists in the construction; the sign flips through the **composition of components** — the discount deepens with every renewal.

**The Hard rating comes from the mechanics of §5**, not from corridor arithmetic: the slot carries a full echelon mechanism of "right first step → wrong answer."

**Separation from ST-009 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-009 the client names a **means** (subscription) mistaken for an end, and the correct move is to redefine the task before any analysis. Here the client names a **genuine goal**; it is correct and achieved. The error is that it is **intermediate**, and optimising it works against the goal above. ST-009 is a goal replaced by an instrument; ST-026 is a correct goal at the wrong level of the hierarchy.

drills.title: Three Right Out of Five
drills.prompt: Hastreling's operating profit fell by $31.0M over the year while revenue grew 3%. The board asks: **what caused the profit decline?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"FX differences"** | profit falls while revenue grows → "a currency effect" | Per the exhibit the contribution is **$0.0M**: the company operates in a single currency. The branch calls for data that does not exist because the phenomenon does not |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Cost of goods** | "input costs" · "production costs" · "COGS" | One of the components; without it the conclusion is unsupported |
| **2. Customer base and its changes** | "who buys" · "customer loss" · "buyer structure" | Without it you cannot connect revenue growth to a profit decline |
| **3. Realised price against list** | "actual transaction price" · "discounts and rebates" · "list-to-net gap" · "net price realisation" | **Load-bearing, and its absence devalues the rest.** Revenue is booked at list; volume rebates are paid at period end and are invisible in the transaction price. Without this branch the causes found explain **the smaller part** of the gap, while the conclusion about priorities is built on them |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — costs vs buyers |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** — costs vs price |

| FX ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial — imported inputs; warning |
| FX ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Cost of goods | input purchase price · unit cost |
| 2. Customer base | departed customers and their former contribution · trend by segment |

key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **size of effect** | branch 3 | the list-to-net gap is usually the largest and the least visible |
| speed of verification | branch 2 | customer departures show up in sales at once |
| cost of data | branch 1 | purchase prices are internal |

But three correct causes deliver **$12.4M of $31.0M = 40.0%**. The fourth, absent from the tree, delivers **$18.6M = 60.0%** — more than all the others combined.

**Top echelon: YES.** The candidate is wrong about none of the causes found — he is wrong about **priorities**, because he ranks them inside 40% of the gap.

**Separation from ST-001 and ST-017 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **ST-001 (a)** | an **explanatory** branch is missing — regulation explains *why* revenue falls; without it the candidate does not understand the cause at all |
| **ST-017 (b)** | a **constraining** branch is missing — the causes are found correctly, but the recommendation cannot be executed |
| **ST-027 (c)** | a branch is missing that **devalues the rest** — the causes are correct, executable, and immaterial: 40% against 60% |

In (a) what was found is wrong. In (b) what was found is right and unrealisable. In (c) what was found is right, realisable, and **does not solve the problem**.

drills.title: In the Wrong Branch
drills.prompt: Juniverre's customer acquisition cost has risen from $34.30 to $51.22 over the year. The board asks: **why is CAC rising, and what should we do?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Auctions got more expensive"** | CAC rises → "clicks cost more" | Per the exhibit the click price rose 8.3% while CAC rose 49.3%. The branch is **relevant** and explains a small part; a candidate who stops there closes the question incorrectly |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Traffic price** | "cost per click" · "cost per impression" · "auction bids" | The first component of the funnel; without it you cannot separate dearer traffic from falling conversion |
| **2. Funnel conversion** | "click-to-purchase" · "funnel efficiency" · "step conversion" | The second component; CAC = traffic price / conversion, so without it the conclusion is unsupported |
| **3. Acquisition channel mix** | "channel mix" · "where they come from" · "source shares" | Without it, rising CAC and a **shift towards an expensive channel** are indistinguishable |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — entry price vs the share who complete |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **double-coverage risk** — click price differs by channel. Separated: branch 1 is price **within** a channel; branch 3 is the **weights** of channels |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ same for conversion |
| Auctions ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **duplicate** — this is branch 1 |
| Auctions ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Traffic price | cost per click by channel, both years |
| 2. Conversion | click-to-purchase conversion by channel, both years |
| 3. Channel mix | channel shares of acquired customers, both years |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| size of effect | branch 2 | conversion usually has more dispersion than click price |
| **speed of verification** | branch 3 | channel shares are visible in the report at once and reframe the picture |
| cost of data | branch 1 | platform data is at hand |
key.s0:
The candidate looks for the cause in branches 1 and 2 — click price and conversion. The price **did rise**, so the branch is not mistaken.

But its contribution is small: within each channel CAC rose 1.6–8.3%, while blended CAC rose 49.3%. The main driver is the **third branch** — the affiliate share grew from 12% to 42%, and that is the most expensive channel ($81.25 against $32.50 and $26.00).

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who checks the channel mix reaches the right answer. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-008 and ST-019 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **ST-008 (a)** | the data **nullifies** the branch — premium is 2.9% of volume and physically cannot deliver the target |
| **ST-019 (b)** | the data **reverses the sign** — the growth driver turns out to be a source of loss |
| **ST-028 (c)** | the data **relocates** the problem — the branch is correct and small, the cause sits in the neighbouring one; the tree needs **reweighting**, not rebuilding |

In (a) the branch disappears. In (b) it changes direction. In (c) it remains correct, but its weight turns out to be the wrong one.

drills.title: Right Number, Wrong Inference
drills.prompt: The development director: "Our premium format delivers $318 of revenue per square metre against $241 for the base format — 32% more. Check the calculation and confirm it: we are rolling the premium format out across the chain."
drills.anchor_metric: premium against base = +32.0% revenue per sq m

exhibit.title: Weldrancy: format comparison
rows: Revenue per sq m **in top-quintile locations**

key.decoy:
| Suspicion | Why it's plausible | What refutes it |
|---|---|---|
| **"An error in the percentage"** | the first thing anyone checks | 318 / 241 − 1 = **+32.0%**; the calculation is correct |
| **"Different bases: not all stores are counted"** | the classic comparability trap | All 228 stores of the chain are included: 42 premium and 186 base |
| **"The periods are not comparable"** | the classic D6 | Both formats are measured over the same full year |

**The key difference from ST-010 and ST-020:** the data is clean **and** the question is legitimate **and** the calculation is correct. The right behaviour is to **confirm all three** and show that **the inference does not follow from the correct number**.
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Correctness of the calculation** | "check the arithmetic" · "is the percentage right" · "does it reconcile" | The director explicitly asks for a check; without it the answer is incomplete |
| **2. Comparability of the groups being compared** | "are the formats comparable" · "how else do they differ" · "are other conditions equal" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** The revenue difference may be explained not by format but by a third variable on which the groups differ. "The format delivers +32%" is unsupported without this check |
| **3. What the conclusion means for the decision** | "does the result transfer" · "what happens on rollout" · "applicability" | Without it you cannot say whether the effect will repeat in the remaining stores |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — was it computed correctly vs what is being compared |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — comparability determines transferability. Separated: branch 2 is **how the groups differ now**; branch 3 is **what happens on rollout** |
| Suspicions ↔ 1 | the suspicions are tested inside branch 1 and are not branches in their own right |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Calculation | 318 / 241 − 1 · store coverage in the calculation · period comparability |
| 2. Comparability | distribution of stores by location footfall · store age · like-for-like comparison in identical locations |
| 3. Applicability | how many chain locations are comparable to the premium ones · effect on rollout into the rest |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| the client's explicit request | branch 1 | the director asked for the calculation to be checked |
| **soundness of the inference** | branch 2 | a correct calculation does not make the inference correct |
| irreversibility | branch 3 | a format rollout is major capex |
key.s0:
A triple inversion, and the subtlest Clean slot in the type.

- The data is clean — all 228 stores, one period, everything shown.
- The question is legitimate — the director asks exactly what is needed for the decision.
- The calculation is correct — 318/241 − 1 = +32.0%, no error.

And **the inference does not follow from the correct number**: 95.2% of premium stores sit in top-quintile footfall locations against 1.1% of base stores. Format and location are **confounded**, and in comparable locations the gap is **+4.3%**, not +32.0%.

**Top echelon: NO** — by construction, this is Clean.

**Separation from ST-010 and ST-020 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | What is clean | What is dirty | The right answer ends with |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ST-010** S16(a) | the data | nothing in the table; the cause is outside it | "look **outside this table**" |
| **ST-020** S16(b) | the data | the **framing of the question** — the metric does not answer the objective | "don't look for **this**" |
| **ST-029** S16(c) | the data, the question **and the calculation** | the **inference from the calculation** — the variables are confounded | "the number is right, **the inference does not follow**" |

In (a) the candidate confirms and sends the search elsewhere. In (b) he reframes the question. In (c) he confirms **everything he was asked to** and shows that what he confirmed provides no basis for the decision.

drills.title: The Same People Twice
drills.prompt: Zabrinth is reviewing customer base growth: +38 thousand customers over the year. The board asks: **where did the growth come from, and is it durable?**

exhibit.title: Zabrinth: customer base growth, thousands of customers

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"The advertising worked"** | base growth → "marketing" | The branch calls for campaign data. Even if confirmed, it does not answer the second half of the question — **is the growth durable** — for which the structure of the increase is needed, not its source |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Inflow: where they came from** | "acquisition sources" · "arrival channels" · "who came in" | Without it the growth cannot be linked to anything the company did |
| **2. Outflow: who left** | "base losses" · "closed accounts" · "churn" | Net growth is a difference; without outflow the durability conclusion is unsupported |
| **3. The cut of the increase — **one and only one** | "how we slice the increase" · "the segmentation base" · "the division criterion" | **Load-bearing.** The increase can be cut by source, by product, by arrival date — but those cuts **overlap**, and added as parallel branches they double-count |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — arrivals and departures do not overlap |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **shared-criterion risk** — "sources" is one of the cuts of the increase. Separated: branch 1 is the **fact** of acquisition; branch 3 is the **choice** of a single cut for the analysis |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ same |
| "New customers" ↔ "Customers who arrived this year" | ⚠️ **IDENTITY** — one set named twice. **The slot's main check** |
| Advertising ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **absorption** — an advertising channel sits inside sources |
| Advertising ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Inflow | customers acquired by source · their first product |
| 2. Outflow | accounts closed · tenure of those who left |
| 3. The cut | **one** chosen criterion · a check that the subsets do not overlap |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **structural correctness** | branch 3 | until the cut is chosen, the sums do not reconcile |
| size of effect | branch 1 | inflow usually exceeds outflow in absolute terms |
| speed of verification | branch 2 | closed accounts are visible at once |
key.s0:
The candidate builds "new customers" and "customers who arrived this year" as parallel branches — for example, separating "acquisition" from "base dynamics." These are **one set**, named twice.

Unlike ST-011, where the overlap is **spatial** and is found by comparing two tables, here it is **temporal** and therefore less visible: both phrasings sound like different categories while describing the same people.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who checks the sums discovers the double count. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-011 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-011 the shared criterion is **spatial** — geography and customer type coincide because chains exist only in cities; it is found arithmetically (34+28=62). Here the shared criterion is **temporal** — "new" and "arrived this year" are identical by the definition in the note, and it is found by **reading the definition**, not by adding. Different origins and different means of detection.

- compute: outflow of 23 thousand against inflow of 61 thousand means gross churn consumes **37.7%** of acquisition
- propose looking at net growth by arrival cohort rather than in aggregate

drills.title: Data About a Different Market
drills.prompt: Belmirov is planning to enter institutional catering (schools, hospitals, the military) — a segment the company **has never worked in**. The board has approved a market entry plan spanning **6 months**. The question: **will the plan work?**

exhibit.title: Belmirov: institutional catering against our current segments
rows: Time to obtain the certificate, months

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST]: which question it fails to answer |
|---|---|---|
| **"Structure of past sales and what worked in it"** | the most natural branch for an entry question: "let's look at what we're good at" | The branch is relevant to the **question** (we really are entering a market) and to the **level of decision** (strategic). But the data it calls for describes **three segments, none of which is the target one**: retail 68%, small HoReCa 22%, online 10%, institutional **0%**. The company has never sold there. The branch returns a complete and accurate picture of a market **the decision does not concern**. It is a branch of a **different period**: it answers "what was" when "what will be" was asked |
| **"Do we have the manufacturing capacity"** | the second reflex | Capacity is sufficient by the terms of the prompt; the branch is refuted by the setup |

**Scored through ORDER.** Naming past sales as the first branch and working it → prioritisation defect. Naming it as a source of hypotheses about our own capabilities and moving on → no defect. Noting oneself that it holds no data on the target segment → **bonus**.
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The segment's entry requirements** | "eligibility conditions" · "what is needed to sell here" · "barriers to entry" · "certification" | Without it you cannot say whether we can sell there at all. "We enter" is unsupported while the requirements are unknown |
| **2. How buying works in the segment** | "how they purchase" · "tender or direct sale" · "deal cycle" · "who decides" | **Load-bearing.** The institutional segment buys through tenders; **the cycle length determines whether the timeline is feasible**. A conclusion about a 6-month plan is unsupported without the deal cycle |
| **3. Contract economics** | "what we will earn" · "contract size and margin" · "segment unit economics" | Without it you cannot judge whether entry is worth the cost, even if entry is possible |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — certification is a condition of eligibility **for a tender**. Separated: branch 1 is **what is required of us**; branch 2 is **how the procedure works**. A candidate who merges them loses the cycle length |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** — eligibility vs money |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — contract size is set by the tender. Separated: branch 2 is the **procedure**; branch 3 the **magnitudes** |
| Past sales ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — historical margin as a reference point; warning |
| Past sales ↔ 1, 2 | **independent** |
| Capacity ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial |
| Capacity ↔ 1, 2 | **independent** |
| Past sales ↔ Capacity | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Requirements | list of mandatory certificates · lead time for each · whether we hold them |
| 2. Buying mechanics | share of purchases through tender · cycle length from announcement to delivery · frequency of announcements |
| 3. Economics | average contract size · segment margin · cost of bidding |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **feasibility** | branch 2 | if the cycle is longer than the plan, nothing else matters |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | the list of requirements is public |
| size of effect | branch 3 | economics determine whether entry is worth it |

**Starting with past sales is an ORDER defect:** the branch holds no data on the target segment.
key.s0:
The "structure of past sales" branch is neither an error nor a foolish move. It is strategic, relevant to the entry question, and returns accurate data. A candidate starting there gets a detailed picture of three segments and **not one fact about the fourth**, because the company's share there is 0%.

E-after shows the decision turns on two rows that do not exist in past experience at all: a tender cycle of **14 months** and a certificate taking **9 months**, sequentially — a minimum of **23 months** against a plan of **6**.

**Top echelon: YES.** The tree is correct, the branch is relevant, the data is accurate — and the answer is wrong.

**Separation from ST-002 and ST-018 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **ST-002 (a)** | the branch is irrelevant to the **question** — "Financials" hold no variable explaining trust |
| **ST-018 (b)** | relevant to the question, but at a **different level of decision** — operations for a strategic question |
| **ST-031 (c)** | relevant to the question and the level, but for a **different period**: it describes a market the decision does not concern |

In (a) the data is not about it. In (b) the data is about it but for another decision. In (c) the data is about it and for it — but **about the past**, while the question is about a market where we have never been.

drills.title: Time Already Spent
drills.prompt: Coventhal has spent two years building its own analytics platform. It is 60% complete. A ready-made product has appeared on the market at $180k a year on subscription. The board asks: **continue development or switch to the ready-made product?**
drills.anchor_metric: two years of development invested, 60% complete

exhibit.title: Coventhal: own platform against the ready-made product
rows: Support of our own solution, per year
Subscription to the ready-made product, per year
Migration to the ready-made product, one-off
Implementation time for the ready-made product, months

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"We've already put in two years — we can't abandon it"** | the strongest intuition, and unlike money, time **feels** irrecoverable in a different way | The two years are **already spent and do not return under either decision**. The branch calls for effort data and progress to date — which are **identical for both options** and therefore cannot distinguish them. *Grounding: Darden 24-25 Sticky Surfactants — "should not bring acquisition price into account, as it is a sunk cost"* |
| **"The team will be demoralised"** | the organisational reflex | A real factor, but it belongs to the **execution** of the decision rather than its selection; in the choice tree it occupies space without supplying a criterion |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Cost to finish** | "what remains to spend" · "remaining work" · "forward cost" | Without it the options cannot be compared: what is relevant is the **remaining** 40%, not the 60% already covered |
| **2. Cost to switch** | "subscription cost" · "migration" · "price of the alternative" | The other half of the comparison |
| **3. Differences in the outcome** | "what we get in each case" · "functionality" · "vendor dependence" | Without it only costs are compared and the outcomes are assumed identical. The conclusion is unsupported if our own solution delivers something the ready-made one does not |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — two mutually exclusive paths |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — the volume of remaining work determines the functionality. Separated: branch 1 is **money and time**; branch 3 is the **properties of the result** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ same for the ready-made product |
| Sunk time ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **direct violation** — past effort inside a branch of future costs. **The slot's main check** |
| Sunk time ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| Team ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — team capability affects the outcome; warning |
| Team ↔ 1, 2 | **independent** |
| Sunk time ↔ Team | ⚠️ partial — "the effort would be wasted" and "the team will be upset" get conflated |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Finishing | person-months for the remaining 40% · time to release · annual support cost |

drills.prompt: Draskilde's gross profit fell by $4.6M. The board asks: **which segment will you start the analysis with, and why?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Start with the largest segment by revenue"** | "size of effect" — the right criterion applied to the familiar quantity | The criterion is right. The quantity is not: the question concerns **profit**, and ranking by revenue produces a different order. The segment with the highest revenue delivers the **third largest** contribution to the profit decline |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Segment contributions to the profit decline** | "who dropped the profit" · "decomposition by segment" · "contribution to the gap" | Without a ranking **on the target quantity**, the prioritisation is unsupported |
| **2. Within a segment: price or volume** | "what fell inside" · "segment decomposition" · "driver of the decline" | Without it the cause is unsupported |
| **3. The prioritisation criterion and its quantity** | "what we rank on" · "which metric carries the priority" · "does the criterion fit the question" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** The criterion "largest contribution" must be applied to the quantity the question is about |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — between segments vs inside one |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — both concern ranking. Separated: branch 1 is the **result** of the ranking; branch 3 is the **choice of quantity** for it |
| 2 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| Revenue ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **the same operation on a different quantity** — placed in parallel, the candidate obtains two contradictory rankings and does not choose between them |
| Revenue ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Contribution | gross profit decline by segment, $M and % of the gap |
| 2. Within a segment | Δ price · Δ volume · Δ margin |
| 3. Criterion | the target quantity of the question · whether the ranking matches it |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **size of effect on profit** | the Roofing segment | $2.4M of $4.6M = 52.2% |
| speed of verification | branch 3 | matching the criterion to the question is one step |
| cost of data | branch 1 | segment data is internal |

drills.title: Both Possible, Not Together
drills.prompt: Ferronwick is planning next year's investments. The budget is **$26.0M** and **8** engineers are free. The board asks: **which projects do we launch?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Rank by NPV and take from the top"** | the standard selection procedure | The ranking is correct and applies **to independent** projects. Here the projects **share a constrained resource**, and the two best by NPV cannot be run together. The branch produces an order from which **no set can be assembled** |
| **"Check each project pays back"** | the second reflex | All three pay back individually; the check eliminates nothing |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Return on each project** | "NPV" · "project economics" · "what we earn" | Without return the choice is unsupported |
| **2. Resources each project needs** | "what it consumes" · "capex and people" · "what it requires" | Without resources feasibility cannot be checked |
| **3. Compatibility of the projects with each other** | "can they run together" · "competition for a resource" · "mutual exclusivity" · "a set, not a list" | **Load-bearing.** The projects are feasible **individually** and some of them are not **together**. "Take the two best by NPV" is unsupported until their compatibility is checked |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — return vs consumption |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** — magnitude of return vs compatibility |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **absorption risk** — compatibility is **determined by** resources. Separated: branch 2 is the resources of **one** project against the limit; branch 3 is the resources of a **set** against the limit. A candidate who checks only branch 2 obtains three feasible projects and an infeasible set |
| NPV ranking ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **duplicate** — this is branch 1 |
| NPV ranking ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **opposition** — a ranking presupposes independence, and branch 3 tests it |
| Payback ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **absorption** |
| Payback ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Return | NPV of each project |
| 2. Resources | capex · engineers required |
| 3. Compatibility | **sum of resources for every subset** against both limits |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **feasibility of the set** | branch 3 | the set, not the list, is the subject of the decision |
| size of effect | branch 1 | NPV establishes what we would like to take |
| speed of verification | branch 2 | each project's resources are known at once |

**A+C requires 12.0 engineers against 8.0 available.** Capex is within limits (23.0 ≤ 26.0) — the constraint binds on the second resource, not on money.

The best feasible set is **A+B** with an NPV of **$15.0M**, even though B is the weakest project by NPV.

**Top echelon: YES.** The tree is complete, every project passes its check, the ranking is correct — and the answer is wrong, because what was checked were **projects** while the decision is about a **set**.

**Separation from ST-007 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-007 the requirement was to name **≥2 options** — the defect was an incomplete list. Here the list is complete, all three are named and checked; the defect is that the options are **not independent**, and checking each individually does not answer the question about a set. There, elements were missing; here, what is missing is a check of the **relations** between them.

drills.title: We Are Not Losing
drills.prompt: The commercial director: "Our revenue fell 11.8% over the year. Find where we are losing position."

key.decoy:
| Suspicion | Why it's plausible | What refutes it |
|---|---|---|
| **"Competitors took share"** | revenue falls → "we're being squeezed" | Market share **did not change**: 13.00% in both years |
| **"We lost major customers"** | the second reflex | The decline is proportional across every segment, with a spread of 0.1 pp |
| **"We had to cut prices"** | the third | Average price did not move; the decline is entirely volume, as it is for the market |

**The difference from ST-010, ST-020 and ST-029 — see §DUP.** Here the data is clean, the question is legitimate, the calculation is correct, and the inference **does** follow — it simply turns out to be "there is no problem."
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. External: market dynamics** | "the market overall" · "industry backdrop" · "category demand" | Without the market you cannot separate our problem from a general one. "We are losing position" is unsupported if everyone fell |
| **2. Internal: our share** | "market share" · "our result relative to the market" · "position" | Without share, the external decline explains everything and internal causes go unexamined |
| **3. What the result means for the question** | "does this answer the director's question" · "is there a problem" · "what do we do with the answer" | **Load-bearing in a Clean slot.** If share is stable, the question "where are we losing position" **has no answer**, because no position has been lost. The correct behaviour is to say so, not to search |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — share is the ratio of ours to the market. Separated: branch 1 is the **absolute** market trend; branch 2 is the **relative** position. Merging into "demand fell" is the S12 error |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — the result on share determines the answer. Separated: branch 2 is the **measurement**; branch 3 the **interpretation for the question** |
| Suspicions ↔ 1, 2 | tested inside the branches; not branches in their own right |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Market | market volume, both years · trend at comparable players |
| 2. Share | our share, both years · decomposition of the decline into market and share effects |
| 3. Interpretation | whether our rate diverges from the market's · what follows from that for the question |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **speed of verification** | branch 1 | industry statistics are public |
| size of effect | branch 2 | share closes the question in one step |
| cost of data | branch 2 | internal sales are free |
key.s0:
The director asks where **we are losing position**, and the question carries an unstated assumption that position is being lost. The candidate performs exactly the operation the S12 focus trains — separating external from internal — and finds: **the market −11.85%, us −11.83%, share 13.00% → 13.00%**.

No position has been lost. We are falling precisely with the market.

**Top echelon: NO** — by construction, this is Clean. An honest **Medium**.

**Clean mechanics:** the focus muscle is **not weakened** — separating external from internal is mandatory and is the only route to the answer. The result of that separation happens to be "clean."

**Separation from every Clean slot ([DUP], against THREE):**

| | What is clean | What is dirty | The right answer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ST-010** S16(a) | the data | nothing in the table; the cause lies outside | "look **outside this table**" |
| **ST-020** S16(b) | the data | the **framing**: the metric does not answer the objective | "don't look for **this**" |
| **ST-029** S16(c) | data, question, calculation | the **inference**: variables are confounded | "the number is right, **the inference does not follow**" |
| **ST-035** S12(b) `○` | data, question, calculation **and inference** | the **premise** of the question: there is no loss | "**there is nothing to look for**, no position has been lost" |

The first three sit in focus S16, whose muscle is "reject false suspicions." Here the focus is **S12**, the muscle is "separate ours from the market's," and Clean arises as the **result** of applying it rather than as the subject of the slot.

drills.title: Sequence Decides
drills.prompt: Lorvantis introduced a new service protocol: savings on the labour hour. Over the year the programme delivered **−$3.0M**. The finance director proposes shutting it down. The board asks: **do we shut it down?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Benchmark competitors: the protocol works for them"** | benchmarking as an argument | The branch calls for data on competitors' protocols. Even complete, it **says nothing about what happens to our economics over time** — and the decision depends on exactly that |
| **"Recompute the labour-hour saving more precisely"** | "perhaps we counted it wrong" | The saving is computed correctly and is stable: **+$1.8M/month** across all twelve months. The branch is refuted by the first row of the exhibit |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. What the programme delivers** | "savings" · "the positive effect" · "gain from the protocol" | Without it you cannot say whether the mechanism works at all |
| **2. What the programme costs** | "customer churn" · "side effect" · "price of the protocol" | Without it only the gain is counted |
| **3. When each effect begins** | "what starts when" · "sequence of effects" · "timing profile" · "in what order" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** The saving starts in month 1, the churn in month 7. The annual total adds effects that began at **different times**, and the sign of that total depends on where the line is drawn. "−$3.0M, shut it down" is unsupported without the timing profile |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — the gain and its price |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — the saving has its own timing profile. Separated: branch 1 is the **magnitude** of the effect; branch 3 is the **moment** it begins. A candidate who runs the profile inside branch 1 loses the comparison of two profiles |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ the same for churn |
| Benchmark ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial — another firm's saving as a reference; warning |
| Benchmark ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| Recompute ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **duplicate** — this is branch 1 |
| Recompute ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| Benchmark ↔ Recompute | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Gain | labour-hour saving, $/month · stability across months |
| 2. Price | customer churn, $/month · share of customers lost · reason for leaving |
| 3. Timing | month each effect begins · **cumulative total by month** · month the sign changes |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **irreversibility** | branch 3 | shutting down is irreversible, and the sign depends on the horizon |
| size of effect | branch 2 | churn exceeds the saving on a monthly basis |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | the saving comes straight out of the job cards |
key.s0:
The candidate builds a correct tree: what it delivers, what it costs, what the total is. All three branches are confirmed by the data, and the annual result of −$3.0M is computed correctly.

The error is that the **horizon is collapsed to a point**. The effects begin in a different order: the saving in month 1, the churn in month 7 — and not by accident, but because customers leave when a six-month package expires. The cumulative total is **positive for ten months** and reaches **+$10.8M** by month 6.

**Top echelon: YES.** The tree is complete, the calculation correct, and the answer "shut it down" is wrong without first asking whether the churn is fixable.

**The sign flip is honest.** There is no discounting and no rate exists in the construction. The sign flips through the **order in which the effects arrive**.

**Separation from ST-016 ([DUP], substantive):** in ST-016 the **horizon** is collapsed — year 1 against three years, and different horizons give different signs. Here the horizon is a single year and what is collapsed is the **sequence within it**: two effects starting in different months. In ST-016 you must look further; in ST-036 you must look **inside**.

drills.title: One Set, Two Names
drills.prompt: Nystrelde's revenue grew 8% while gross profit fell 3%. **Build a tree to find the cause.**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Costs went up"** | profit falls while revenue grows → "costs" | The branch is **relevant and far too broad**: it contains everything and gives no direction. It calls for a full cost structure from which, without a customer cut, no conclusion follows |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Customer base structure** | "customer types" · "buyer segments" · "who buys from us" | Without a customer cut you cannot connect revenue growth to a margin decline |
| **2. Margin by contract type** | "contract terms" · "margin by contract type" · "volume discounts" | Without it the cause of the margin decline is unsupported |
| **3. Checking that the chosen cuts do not overlap** | "is this one division" · "are the branches independent" · "are we counting twice" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** "Corporate customers" and "customers on an annual contract" sound like two different cuts, and by the company's own definition they are **one set**. A conclusion drawn on the sum of those branches is unsupported |
key.me:
> **This slot trains an absorption invisible in the structure.** The branches are not nested: neither is a sub-branch of the other, both sit at the first level, each has its own sub-branches. The test "is A part of B" **passes** for both. The overlap is found only by comparing **definitions**.

| Pair | Structural nesting | Overlap by definition | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corporate ↔ Annual contract** | **none** — both at the first level | **YES** — corporate status is granted **only** on an annual contract; 218 customers in both branches, and they are the same 218 | ⚠️ **IDENTITY. The slot's main check** |
| Corporate ↔ Retail | none | none | **independent** |
| Annual contract ↔ One-off orders | none | none | **independent** |
| Corporate ↔ One-off orders | none | none | **independent** — by definition a corporate customer places no one-off orders |
| Costs ↔ 2 | none | ⚠️ partial — contract cost sits inside margin | warning |
| Costs ↔ 1, 3 | none | none | **independent** |

**Resolution rule:** before adding branches, compare **definitions**, not the structure of the tree. Two branches defined through one attribute are identical even when they stand side by side.
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Customer base | customer counts by type · their share of revenue |
| 2. Margin by contract | gross margin by contract type · depth of volume discounts |
| 3. Non-overlap | the definition of each attribute · set sizes · a test for identity |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **structural correctness** | branch 3 | until the cuts are checked, the sums do not reconcile |
| size of effect | branch 2 | margin is the subject of the question |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | counts come out of the CRM at once |
key.s0:
The candidate sets "corporate customers" and "customers on an annual contract" as two branches. **Structurally they are not nested** — both at the first level, each with its own sub-branches, and the nesting test passes.

The overlap exists only in the **definition**: corporate status in this company is granted **on signing an annual contract**, so these are exactly the same 218 customers. Added as parallel branches, they give 436 customers against an actual 218.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who compares definitions discovers the identity. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-003 and ST-021 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | Where the overlap sits | How it is detected |
|---|---|---|
| **ST-003 (a)** | **within one level** — "fuel" beside "variable costs" | a pairwise check of the first level |
| **ST-021 (b)** | **between levels** — a sub-branch of one branch is another first-level branch | a two-level matrix |
| **ST-037 (c)** | **in the definition** — there is no structural nesting at all | comparing **definitions**, not structure |

In (a) and (b) the overlap is visible from the tree. In (c) the tree is correct under any structural check, and it can be found only by reading what each attribute means.

| **ST-021 (b)** | **between levels** — a sub-branch of one branch is another first-level branch | a two-level matrix |
| **ST-037 (c)** | **in the definition** — there is no structural nesting at all | comparing **definitions**, not structure |

In (a) and (b) the overlap is visible from the tree. In (c) the tree is correct under any structural check, and it can be found only by reading what each attribute means.

- propose checking whether volume discounts are tied to annual contracts
- observe that the identity of the branches makes any addition of them invalid

drills.title: The Chain Is Profitable, the Stores Are Not
drills.prompt: The Roquemarn chain is profitable: **$163.5M** of operating profit. The board is considering closing loss-making sites. The question: **is there anything to close, and how do we choose?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"The chain is profitable — there is nothing to close"** | a direct inference from the aggregate | The aggregate is correct: $163.5M. But the question concerns **sites**, and chain profit is the **sum across sites**, some of which are loss-making. The branch calls for the consolidated P&L, from which the distribution **cannot be seen by construction** |
| **"Compare the regions"** | the natural next level | All three regions are profitable ($41.2M, $88.6M, $33.7M). A regional cut **does not answer** a question about sites — it sits one level above what is required |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The level at which the decision is taken** | "what are we deciding about" · "unit of decision" · "are we closing a chain, a region or a site" | **Load-bearing.** The decision concerns a **site**, so the data must be at site level. "The chain is profitable" does not answer a question about sites |
| **2. Distribution of the result within a level** | "spread across sites" · "how many are loss-making" · "structure inside the aggregate" | Without the distribution, an aggregate says nothing about the tail |
| **3. Economics of closing a site** | "what closure delivers" · "exit costs" · "revenue transfer to neighbours" | Without it "close the loss-makers" is unsupported: part of the revenue transfers, part of the cost remains |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — both concern the level of the data. Separated: branch 1 is **which level is required** for the decision; branch 2 is **what is visible at it** |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** — level of analysis vs economics of the action |
| 2 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| "Chain is profitable" ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **special case** — an answer at the wrong level |
| "Chain is profitable" ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| Regions ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **special case** — an intermediate level |
| Regions ↔ 2 | ⚠️ partial — a region is a grouping of sites; warning |
| Regions ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| "Chain is profitable" ↔ Regions | ⚠️ both are aggregates at different levels |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Level | unit of decision (the site) · whether data exists at that level |
| 2. Distribution | number of loss-making sites · their share · spread across regions |
| 3. Economics of closure | annual loss per site · share of revenue transferring to neighbours · closure costs |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **fit to the level of the question** | branch 1 | an aggregate does not answer a question about sites |
| size of effect | branch 2 | the distribution shows the scale of the tail |
| irreversibility | branch 3 | closing a site cannot be undone |
key.s0:
The tree "profit = revenue − costs" is right as a type. The unit of analysis is right too — profit, which is what was asked about. What is wrong is the **level of aggregation**: the count is at chain level while the decision is taken at site level.

All three levels are internally consistent and the sums reconcile. And each gives its own answer: the chain is profitable ($163.5M), every region is profitable, **34 of 121 sites are loss-making** — and in the South that is **55.2%** against 19–21% elsewhere.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who asks "at what level is this decision taken" reaches the right answer. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-004 and ST-022 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | What is wrong |
|---|---|
| **ST-004 (a)** | the **type** of tree — a profit tree for a foundation with no revenue |
| **ST-022 (b)** | the **unit of analysis** — margin per customer instead of margin per order |
| **ST-038 (c)** | the **level of aggregation** — the same quantity computed on the chain rather than the site |

In (a) the tree is not about this organisation. In (b) the tree is about it but counts the wrong entity. In (c) the tree is about it and counts the right entity — but **at the wrong level**, and at the required level the answer is the opposite.

drills.title: Stopped Too Early
drills.prompt: Miradelph's costs rose 12% with transport volume unchanged. **Work out the cause.**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Inflation"** | the universal explanation for any cost increase | The branch calls for macro data. Even if confirmed, it **produces no action**: inflation is the same for everyone and does not indicate where our costs specifically rose |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Which line rose** | "cost structure" · "breakdown by line" · "where exactly the growth sits" | Without localisation the analysis has not begun |
| **2. Within the line: price or quantity** | "line decomposition" · "rate or volume" · "what changed inside" | Without it, "logistics got more expensive" is indistinguishable from "we started running more empty miles" |
| **3. What to do with what is found** | "how deep to take the analysis" · "is there an answer yet" · "is this enough for a decision" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** The analysis is complete when the level reached **permits naming an action**. "Logistics rose" gives no action; "the contractor's rate rose 18% at unchanged mileage" does |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — between lines vs inside a line |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — the depth of decomposition determines whether an action exists. Separated: branch 2 is **how** to decompose; branch 3 is **when to stop** |
| Inflation ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial — inflation manifests through the lines; warning |
| Inflation ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **absorption** — inflation is the price component |
| Inflation ↔ 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Lines | Δ for each line, $ and % · contribution to the total increase |
| 2. Within a line | rate · quantity · their product |
| 3. Stopping point | **whether a specific action can be named at this level** |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| size of effect | branch 1 | localisation splits the problem |
| **sufficiency for action** | branch 3 | it determines where to stop |
| cost of data | branch 2 | rate data sits with the contractors |
key.s0:
The tree is complete, depth is allocated correctly, the order is correct: the candidate moves from cost structure to the largest line and finds — "logistics rose." And **stops**, because at that level there is already an answer to "where."

But the question was "work out the cause," and "logistics" is a **place**, not a cause. At that level no action can be named: it is not known whether the contractor's rate rose, mileage rose, or the route mix changed.

**Top echelon: NO** — by construction. An honest **Easy**.

**Separation from ST-013 and ST-024 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | What is wrong |
|---|---|
| **ST-013 (a)** | depth went **into the wrong branch** — three levels inside a line with no contribution |
| **ST-024 (b)** | depth is right, the **order** is wrong — started with staff ($1.1M), never reached fuel ($8.9M) |
| **ST-039 (c)** | depth and order are right, the **stopping point** is wrong — reached the right branch and halted before an action appeared |

In (a) he dug in the wrong place. In (b) he dug in the right place in the wrong order. In (c) he dug in the right place in the right order — and **abandoned it halfway**.

drills.title: Right Participant, Wrong Moment
drills.prompt: Ulmaverd is building a new production line. The plan runs 20 months on a budget of $23.6M. The board asks: **is the plan realistic?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"The contractors will miss the deadlines"** | the classic construction risk | The branch calls for contractor track records. The data exists and does not bear on the defect in the plan: the problem is not in executing the stages but in their **order** |
| **"The budget is understated"** | the second reflex | The budget matches the stage estimates; the issue is not magnitude |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Composition of the stages** | "what the project contains" · "stages" · "completeness of the plan" | Without completeness the plan cannot be judged |
| **2. Participants and their roles** | "who is involved" · "stakeholders" · "whose sign-off is needed" | Without participants the points of external dependency are invisible |
| **3. The moment each participates** | "when each is engaged" · "sequence and dependencies" · "what must precede what" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** The regulator **is** in the plan and the role is stated correctly — approval. But it sits **after** installation, while approval is required **before procurement**: equipment cannot be specified without knowing the requirements. A conclusion about the plan's realism is unsupported without checking the order |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — what we do vs who is involved |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — stages have an order by definition. Separated: branch 1 is the **list** of stages; branch 3 is the **dependencies** between them and the participants |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **role/moment conflation risk** — the key pair of the slot. Separated: branch 2 is **what** a participant does; branch 3 is **when** their involvement is required. A candidate who checks only branch 2 finds the regulator and finds no defect |
| Contractors ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial — execution of the stages; warning |
| Contractors ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **absorption** — a contractor is a participant |
| Contractors ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| Budget ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial |
| Budget ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| Contractors ↔ Budget | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Stages | the list · duration of each · sum against the plan |
| 2. Participants | the list · role of each · right to block |
| 3. Moments | month of involvement per the plan · **month at which involvement is required** · the difference |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **irreversibility** | branch 3 | procurement before approval creates write-off risk |
| size of effect | branch 3 | a shift in order changes the whole schedule |
| speed of verification | branch 2 | the list of participants is known |
key.s0:
The regulator **is named** in the plan. The role is stated **correctly** — project approval. The standard check "are all participants accounted for and their functions correctly described" **passes in full**.

The defect is in the **moment**: approval sits in months 15–20 while it is required **before month 5**, because it determines the equipment requirements. Procurement of $8.4M happens in months 5–9 — that is, **before** the requirements are known, and non-returnable if they are not met.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who checks the dependencies between stages finds the defect. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-014 and ST-025 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | Actor | Role | Moment | What is detected |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ST-014 (a)** | **not named** | — | — | a missing row in the map |
| **ST-025 (b)** | named | **wrong** (channel instead of decision-maker) | — | a mismatch between two role columns |
| **ST-040 (c)** | named | **correct** | **wrong** | a mismatch between two **date** columns |

Three states: the actor is absent; the actor is present but does the wrong thing; the actor is present and does the right thing — but **not at the right time**.
