drills.title: Divided by the Consequence
drills.prompt: Blythmore serves **379** customers. Margin has fallen for three years. The board asks: **which customers are we losing money on, and what do we do about them?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Cost of service has risen"** | margin falls → costs | Per the exhibit the cost of serving a customer is **stable** across all three groups. The branch calls for a cost structure and is refuted by the first row |
| **"Competitors are undercutting us"** | the second reflex | Average invoice is not falling; the branch is refuted by the data |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The cut of the customer base** | "customer segmentation" · "how we slice the base" · "customer structure" | Without **one non-contradictory** cut the loss cannot be located |
| **2. Customer economics within the cut** | "margin per customer" · "segment profitability" · "what each brings" | Without economics a cut yields no conclusion |
| **3. Checking the independence of the cut's attributes** | "are the attributes independent" · "does one follow from the other" · "is this one division" | **Load-bearing.** The attribute "profitable" may be a **consequence** of the attribute "large" if profitability is determined by size. Then it is one division named twice, and adding the branches doubles the base |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — how we slice vs what is inside |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — both concern the cut. Separated: branch 1 is the **choice** of attribute; branch 3 is the **check** that the attributes do not follow from one another |
| 2 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| **"Large" ↔ "Profitable"** | ⚠️ **IDENTITY BY CONSEQUENCE.** The profitability threshold of $500k coincides with the boundary of "large." There is no structural nesting and the definitions differ — the **sets** coincide: 64 = 64. **The slot's main check** |
| Cost of service ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **absorption** — costs sit inside customer economics |
| Cost of service ↔ 1, 3 | **independent** |
| Competitors ↔ 2 | ⚠️ partial — a competitor's price affects the invoice; warning |
| Competitors ↔ 1, 3 | **independent** |
| Cost of service ↔ Competitors | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. The cut | customers by the chosen attribute · their share of revenue |
| 2. Economics | revenue and margin per customer by group · cost of service |
| 3. Independence | **intersection of the sets** under the two attributes · whether one attribute follows from the other |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **structural correctness** | branch 3 | until the cuts are checked, the sums do not reconcile |
| size of effect | branch 2 | margin is the subject of the question |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | counts come from the CRM at once |
key.s0:
The candidate sets "large customers" and "profitable customers" as two branches of the cut. The attributes are **different by definition** — one concerns customer revenue, the other their profitability. There is no structural nesting. The test "is A part of B" passes.

The exhibit, released after, shows: the cost of service **does not depend on size** ($41.0k for any customer), so profitability is determined by the single condition "revenue > $500k" — that is, **the boundary of the "large" group**. The sets coincide: 64 = 64.

**Top echelon: YES.** The tree is correct structurally and by definitions, and still counts the same customers twice.

**Separation from ST-011, ST-030 and ST-037 ([DUP], against ALL THREE):**

| | Nature of the coincidence | How it is detected |
|---|---|---|
| **ST-011** S04(a) | **spatial**: chains exist only in cities | adding the shares (34+28=62) |
| **ST-030** S04(b) | **temporal**: "new" = "arrived this year" | reading the definition |
| **ST-037** S03(c) | **by rule**: corporate status is granted on an annual contract | comparing definitions |
| **ST-041** S04(c) | **by consequence**: profitability **follows** from size through a fixed cost of service | examining the **mechanism** that generates the attribute |

In ST-037 the coincidence is set by a **rule of assignment**. Here it is set by **economics**: nobody defined profitability through size, it came out that way because the cost is fixed.

drills.title: The Word Was Given in Public
drills.prompt: A year ago Kestravel's CEO publicly told investors the company would build its own network of collection points — 200 sites in two years. Sixty are open. The economics are worse than plan. The board asks: **continue or wind down?**

exhibit.title: Kestravel: the collection point network
rows: Return on the alternative project, per annum

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"We announced it publicly — backing out damages our reputation"** | the strongest argument in the room, and it is **not monetary** | The public commitment is **already given and cannot be withdrawn** under either decision: the reputational damage from "announced and did not deliver" was incurred the moment the plan became unachievable, not at the moment of admitting it. The branch calls for an assessment of investor reaction — which is **identical under both options** as regards what has already been said, and therefore does not distinguish them |
| **"We've put in $34M"** | the monetary sunk cost, the familiar trap | The $34M is spent and does not return; a classic sunk cost, discarded by the standard rule |

**The difference between the two decoys is the slot itself.** The second is discarded mechanically by anyone who knows the rule. The first **feels different**, because it is not expressed in money.
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Future economics of the network** | "what it delivers from here" · "flow from the sites we have" · "forward cash flow" | Without future flows the decision is unsupported |
| **2. Cost of completing and cost of exiting** | "how much to reach 200" · "price of winding down" · "cost of each decision" | Without the price of both paths there is no comparison |
| **3. Alternative use of the capital** | "what instead" · "where else to invest" · "opportunity cost" | Without an alternative, "continue" is compared only against "do nothing" |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — flow from what operates vs the cost of changing course |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — capital released on exit is the input to the alternative. Separated: branch 2 is the **outflow** on exit; branch 3 is the **use** of what is released |
| **Reputation ↔ 1** | ⚠️ **substitution risk** — "investors will be disappointed → revenue will fall" converts the non-monetary into the monetary **without data**. Permissible only with an estimate; a bare mention inside the flow branch is a violation |
| Reputation ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
| $34M ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **direct violation** — past cost inside a branch of future costs |
| $34M ↔ 1, 3 | **independent** |
| Reputation ↔ $34M | ⚠️ both are sunk, of different natures |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Future economics | flow per operating site per year · share of sites that have reached payback |
| 2. Cost of decisions | capex for the remaining sites · cost of closing the 60 · lease obligations |
| 3. Alternative | return on the nearest project using the same capital |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| size of effect | branch 2 | the remaining sites are the largest sum in the problem |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | data on the 60 operating sites exists |
| reversibility | branch 3 | capital sunk into the network does not transfer |
key.s0:
The monetary sunk cost ($34.0M) is discarded — the rule is known and the figure is explicit. **The reputational stake is not discarded**, because it does not look like a cost: it looks like an **obligation**, and obligations are meant to be honoured.

Yet it is sunk in exactly the same way: the word has been said, and the damage from a gap between word and deed arises from **the plan being unachievable**, not from admitting it. It does not distinguish between decisions about the future.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who applies the rule to a non-monetary sunk item reaches the right answer. An honest **Medium**.

**Separation from ST-005 and ST-032 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | What is sunk | Why it is disguised |
|---|---|---|
| **ST-005** S06(a) | **money** — $40M for a line | not disguised at all; the rule in its plain form |
| **ST-032** S06(b) | **time and position** — 2 years, 60% complete | "we're almost done" sounds like an argument for |
| **ST-042** S06(c) | a **public commitment** | it looks not like a cost but like an **obligation**; backing out feels like breaking one's word |

In (a) the figure is monetary and explicit. In (b) it is non-monetary but expressible as cost. In (c) it is non-monetary and **not expressible as cost at all** — it concerns reputation, and the sunk-cost rule is hardest of all to apply to it.

In (a) the figure is monetary and explicit. In (b) it is non-monetary but expressible as cost. In (c) it is non-monetary and **not expressible as cost at all** — it concerns reputation, and the sunk-cost rule is hardest of all to apply to it.

- notice that 19 of 60 sites have not reached payback — **31.7%** of those already open
- compute: $71.0M at the 14% alternative gives **$9.94M/year** of forgone return
- propose a third path: stop at 60 sites, neither closing nor expanding

drills.title: The Metric Does Not Choose
drills.prompt: Orvenmark is choosing a site for a distribution centre from three options. The logistics director: "We measure on average delivery cost per order — that is our headline metric. Check the calculation and name the winner."

key.decoy:
| Suspicion | Why it's plausible | What refutes it |
|---|---|---|
| **"The calculation is wrong — they cannot be identical"** | three different sites, one identical figure looks suspicious | The calculation is correct: delivery cost is driven by the average haul, and all three sites sit at an **equal distance** from the centre of demand mass. The coincidence is substantive, not an error |
| **"Rounding hides a difference"** | the classic precision trap | The values coincide to the **second decimal**: $4.82 across all three. The note states the precision of the calculation |
| **"Not all orders were included"** | the base of comparison | The full annual volume is included: 1.42 million orders for each site |

**The key difference:** the data is clean, the question is legitimate, the metric is **appropriate** and correctly computed — it simply **does not distinguish between the options**.
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Correctness of the metric's calculation** | "is it computed right" · "check the arithmetic" · "does it reconcile" | The director explicitly asks for a check; without it the answer is incomplete |
| **2. The metric's discriminating power** | "does it distinguish the options" · "can a choice be made on it" · "is there any spread" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** A metric identical across every option **cannot** serve as a selection criterion, however correct it is |
| **3. Metrics on which the options do differ** | "what else differs" · "what to choose on" · "other criteria" | Without them the answer reduces to "no choice can be made," when a choice must be made |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — is it computed right vs is the result useful |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — both concern the fitness of metrics. Separated: branch 2 concerns **this** metric; branch 3 concerns **the others** |
| Suspicions ↔ 1 | tested inside branch 1; not branches in their own right |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Correctness | delivery cost per order for each site · calculation base · precision |
| 2. Discriminating power | **spread of values across the options** · is it sufficient for a choice |
| 3. Other metrics | capex · rent · 24-hour coverage · their spread |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| the client's explicit request | branch 1 | the director asked for the calculation to be checked |
| **fitness for choosing** | branch 2 | a correct metric with no spread does not solve the task |
| size of effect | branch 3 | capex differs by a factor of 1.8 |
key.s0:
A triple inversion, the fourth Clean state in the type.

- The data is clean — all three sites, the full volume, precision to the cent.
- The question is legitimate — a site really does need choosing.
- The metric is **appropriate** — delivery cost is relevant for a distribution centre.
- The calculation is correct — $4.82 is confirmed.

And the metric **does not distinguish the options**: the spread is zero. "The winner is X" cannot be said, not because the data is poor, but because **the chosen criterion is identical for all**.

**Top echelon: NO** — by construction, this is Clean.

**Separation from every Clean slot ([DUP], against FOUR):**

| | What is clean | What prevents an answer | The answer ends with |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ST-010** S16(a) | the data | nothing; the cause lies outside the table | "look **outside this table**" |
| **ST-020** S16(b) | the data | the metric does not answer the **objective** | "don't look for **this**" |
| **ST-029** S16(c) | data, question, calculation | the **inference** does not follow from the number | "the number is right, **the inference does not follow**" |
| **ST-035** S12(b) | data, question, calculation, inference | the **premise** of the question is false | "**there is nothing to look for**" |
| **ST-043** S07(c) | data, question, calculation, **and the metric is appropriate** | the metric **does not discriminate** | "the metric is right, **but you cannot choose on it**" |

In ST-020 the metric is not about the right thing. Here the metric **is** about the right thing and correctly computed — it is simply identical.

drills.title: Not the Largest, the Most Irreversible
drills.prompt: Duskavine has three initiatives and limited management capacity — only one can run at a time. The board asks: **which do we start with?**

exhibit.title: Duskavine: three initiatives
header: What happens if the deadline is missed
rows: rollout slips, the benefit arrives later
the campaign moves to the next season
**production halts until a new licence is issued**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Start with the largest effect"** | the right criterion, correctly applied to the right quantity | The criterion is right, the quantity is right (effect in $M), the ranking is correct: ERP $2.1M → rebranding $1.8M → licence $0.4M. And the priority is **still wrong**: the first two are **reversible**, the third is not, and missing it costs not $0.4M but a production shutdown |
| **"Start with the cheapest"** | resource logic | The cost of the initiatives does not differ materially; the criterion eliminates nothing |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Effect of each initiative** | "what it brings" · "size of the gain" · "economics" | Without the effect the prioritisation is unsupported |
| **2. Deadline and how hard it is** | "when is it needed" · "is there a hard date" · "what happens if it slips" | Without the deadline the order can be assigned arbitrarily |
| **3. Cost of error: is the decision reversible** | "what if we don't do it" · "reversibility" · "price of missing it" · "can we come back" | **Load-bearing.** Priority is set not by the size of the gain but by the **price of missing it**. A reversible initiative can be postponed and resumed; an irreversible one cannot, and its price of omission is not equal to its effect |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — magnitude vs timing |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — "cost of error" looks like part of the effect. Separated: branch 1 is the **gain from doing it**; branch 3 is the **loss from missing it**. They are **not symmetric**: the licence delivers $0.4M and costs production |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — a hard deadline often implies irreversibility. Separated: branch 2 is **when**; branch 3 is **what happens if later** |
| Largest effect ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **duplicate** — this is branch 1 |
| Largest effect ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **opposition** — ranking by effect ignores the asymmetry in the price of omission |
| Cheapest ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial |
| Cheapest ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Effect | $M of annual gain for each initiative |
| 2. Timing | months to the deadline · how hard it is (regulatory / internal) |
| 3. Cost of error | **what happens on missing it** · is it reversible · the size of the loss, which is not the effect |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **irreversibility** | the licence | missing it halts production; the only non-recoverable item |
| deadline | the licence | 3 months against 9 and 14 |
| size of effect | ERP | $2.1M is the largest gain, but reversible |

**Starting with ERP under the criterion "size of effect" is legitimate by the letter and wrong in substance:** the criterion does not account for the asymmetry in the price of omission. An ORDER defect is recorded if the candidate **has not checked** reversibility.
key.s0:
The candidate names the criterion "largest effect" — the right criterion. He applies it to the quantity "effect in $M" — **the right quantity** (unlike ST-033, where the quantity was wrong). The ranking is correct: 2.1 → 1.8 → 0.4.

And the priority is still wrong, because the criterion **measures the wrong thing when setting a sequence**. Under sequential execution what matters is not the gain but the **loss from postponement**, and that is asymmetric: reversible initiatives lose time, the irreversible one loses production.

**Top echelon: YES.** The criterion is right, the quantity is right, the arithmetic is right — and the answer is wrong.

**Separation from ST-012 and ST-033 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | Criterion | Quantity | What is wrong |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ST-012** S08(a) | named | — | the criterion is **violated**: declared "size of effect," went to a line contributing 8% |
| **ST-033** S08(b) | right, honoured | **wrong** — revenue instead of profit | the ranking is on the wrong quantity |
| **ST-044** S08(c) | right, honoured | **right** — effect in $M | **the criterion itself does not fit the task**: sequencing needs the price of omission, not the size of the gain |

Three levels: fail to honour the criterion → honour it on the wrong quantity → honour it on the right quantity **with the wrong criterion**.

- compute: the price of missing the licence is **$45.75M–$71.17M** against its effect of $0.4M — a gap of **114–178 times**

drills.title: The Fourth Door
drills.prompt: Demand for Pellworth's products exceeds capacity by **34%**. The board asks: **how do we add capacity?**

exhibit.title: Pellworth: options for covering the 34% shortfall

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Pick the best of the three options by NPV"** | three options are named, all costed, the ranking is correct | The ranking is correct **within the list**, and the list is **incomplete**: the axis "way of obtaining capacity" admits a fourth option — **do not add it**. The branch calls for the NPV of three options and will not reveal that the comparison needs a fourth |
| **"Check the financing"** | the resource reflex | All three options fit within the limit; the branch eliminates nothing |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Ways of obtaining capacity** | "options" · "alternatives" · "how we could add it" | Without a list there is no choice |
| **2. Economics of each way** | "NPV" · "return" · "what it costs" | Without economics the options are not comparable |
| **3. Completeness of the option space** | "are all the ways named" · "is the list closed" · "is there a do-nothing option" | **Load-bearing.** A list of three ways of **adding** capacity omits the option of not adding it, and per the exhibit that option turns out to be the best. "Build the plant" is unsupported until it has been compared against declining to grow |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — the list vs the economics |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — both concern the list. Separated: branch 1 is **which ways exist**; branch 3 is **whether all ways are there, including refusal** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| NPV of three ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **duplicate** — this is branch 2 applied to an incomplete set |
| NPV of three ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **opposition** — a ranking presupposes the list is complete |
| Financing ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **absorption** |
| Financing ↔ 1, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Ways | the list of options · resources each requires |
| 2. Economics | NPV of each option · time to capacity |
| 3. Completeness | **the axis of enumeration** · whether the set is closed on that axis · whether a "do nothing" option is present |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **completeness of the set** | branch 3 | ranking an incomplete list gives the wrong answer however precise it is |
| size of effect | branch 2 | NPV ranks them |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | the list can be drawn without data |
key.s0:
The question is framed as "**how do we add** capacity," and that framing **narrows the space** to ways of adding. The candidate names three, all correct, all independent, all costed — and the list looks exhaustive, because it exhausts the **stated** axis.

The fourth option lies on the same real axis ("bring demand and capacity into balance") and delivers the **highest NPV of $26.3M at zero capex**.

**Top echelon: YES.** Three options are named correctly, are independent, are costed correctly and ranked correctly — and the answer is wrong, because the set is incomplete.

**Separation from ST-007 and ST-034 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | What is wrong with the options |
|---|---|
| **ST-007** S10(a) | **one** is named, ≥2 required — the list is **quantitatively incomplete** |
| **ST-034** S10(b) | all three are named, independence is **violated** — together they do not fit the resource |
| **ST-045** S10(c) | all are named, independent, costed — the set is **not closed on the axis**: a fourth element exists outside the stated framing |

In (a) elements are missing. In (b) the elements conflict. In (c) the elements are fine, and **the axis of enumeration has been narrowed by the question**.

drills.title: The External Turned Out to Be Ours
drills.prompt: Grenvalt's revenue fell 17.6% over the year. The sales director: "The distributor channel market contracted by a third — that is an external shock, it is not our doing." The board asks: **is that so?**

exhibit.title: Grenvalt: revenue by channel and the market
rows: Our share of the distribution channel

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Confirm the external shock with industry statistics"** | the director has supplied a ready hypothesis with a figure | The statistics **will confirm** the channel contraction: −32.6%, the fact is correct. The branch calls for data that **proves the phenomenon is external** and will not show that it was caused by our own decision. Confirmation of a correct figure under a wrong attribution of cause |
| **"Compare against competitors in the same channel"** | the second reflex | Competitors in the channel grew — the branch will indicate that it is not purely external, but will not explain the mechanism |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. External: market dynamics** | "the market" · "industry backdrop" · "category demand" | Without the market the scale of the external effect cannot be assessed |
| **2. Internal: our share and its movement** | "share" · "our result relative to the market" · "position" | Without share the external explains everything |
| **3. The cause of the "external" itself** | "what caused the contraction" · "who created it" · "our own past decisions" · "the boundary of external" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** A phenomenon assigned to the external may be a **consequence of our own decision**. Then the boundary between external and internal has been drawn wrongly, and "it is not our doing" is unsupported |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — share is the ratio of ours to the market. Separated: branch 1 is the **absolute** trend; branch 2 the **relative** position |
| 1 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **the slot's key pair** — "market dynamics" and "the cause of those dynamics" look like one branch. Separated: branch 1 is **what happened** to the market; branch 3 is **why**, and whether we did it. A candidate who merges them accepts the external as given |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ partial — share loss and our decision are linked. Separated: branch 2 is the **measurement** of position; branch 3 the **causal link** to our action |
| Statistics ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **duplicate** — this is branch 1 |
| Statistics ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **opposition** — confirming a fact is not establishing a cause |
| Competitors ↔ 2 | ⚠️ partial |
| Competitors ↔ 1, 3 | **independent** |
| Statistics ↔ Competitors | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Market | channel volume by year · trend of the whole category |
| 2. Share | our share by channel · our rate against the market's |
| 3. Cause of the external | **dates of our decisions against dates of the contraction** · counterparty behaviour after our action |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **testing the client's hypothesis** | branch 3 | the director has already supplied an attribution; that is what is being tested |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | industry statistics are public |
| size of effect | branch 2 | share shows whether this is our result |
key.s0:
The candidate performs **the correct operation of focus S12** — separating external from internal. And the separation confirms the director's hypothesis: the channel market really did contract 32.6%, an external fact.

The error is in the **boundary**: the channel contraction was caused in part by our own launch of direct sales. The phenomenon has been assigned to the external **by where it is observed**, not by its cause. We fall faster than the channel market (−46.5% against −32.6%), lose share within it (−3.0 pp), and grow in direct sales at the same time (+21.3%).

**Top echelon: YES.** The separation is performed correctly, the external fact is confirmed — and the conclusion "it is not our doing" is wrong.

**Separation from ST-015 and ST-035 ([DUP], against BOTH, same focus S12):**

| | The separation | The result |
|---|---|---|
| **ST-015** S12(a) | **not performed** — "demand fell" conflates ours with the market's | performing it gives the candidate the answer |
| **ST-035** S12(b) `○` | performed correctly | the result is **clean**: share is stable, nothing has been lost — **Clean** |
| **ST-046** S12(c) | performed correctly | the result is **misleading**: the external is confirmed but **we caused it** — the boundary is in the wrong place |

In (a) the operation is not performed. In (b) it is performed and the answer is "there is no problem." In (c) it is performed and the answer looks like "the problem is external" — and that is a **wrong attribution**, not a wrong calculation.

drills.title: Both Optimisations Are Correct
drills.prompt: Yarnstead's board requires profit to be maximised over three years. Management proposes raising prices by 5% — "the best option on profit." The board asks: **is it the best?**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"Check management's calculation"** | the natural first reaction to someone else's recommendation | Management's calculation is **correct**: raising prices delivers the highest profit **in year one** ($16.8M). The branch calls for their workings and will confirm them. The divergence is not in the arithmetic but in the **objective function** |
| **"Assess the competitor response"** | the market reflex | The competitor response is already embedded in the yearly forecasts; the branch duplicates the exhibit |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The result of each option by year** | "profit by year" · "option trajectories" · "what each delivers" | Without a yearly breakdown the objective functions are indistinguishable |
| **2. The board's objective function** | "what the board maximises" · "a three-year horizon" · "the sum over the period" | Without stating the board's objective the comparison is arbitrary |
| **3. Management's objective function and how it differs** | "what management maximises" · "whose objective is being optimised" · "do the parties' objectives coincide" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** Both parties optimise **correctly**, but **different** functions: the board the three-year sum, management the first-year result. "Management made a mistake" is unsupported — they made none, they are solving a different problem |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | **independent** — the data vs the criterion |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **the slot's key pair** — two objective functions on the same data. Separated: branch 2 is the **three-year sum**; branch 3 is the **first year**. A candidate who runs a single "company objective" will not see the divergence |
| Check the calculation ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **duplicate** — this is branch 1 |
| Check the calculation ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **opposition** — a correct calculation does not imply a correct objective |
| Competitors ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **absorption** — the response is embedded in the forecasts |
| Competitors ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Result | profit of each option in each year |
| 2. Board's objective | **the three-year sum** for each option · the optimum |
| 3. Management's objective | **the first year** for each option · the optimum · what sets management's horizon |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **fit to the objective** | branch 2 | without an explicit objective function no comparison is possible |
| size of effect | branch 1 | the data supplies the magnitudes |
| speed of verification | branch 3 | management's horizon follows from the incentive scheme |
key.s0:
Management **is not making a mistake**. Its calculation is correct, the data is agreed, and option C really does deliver the highest first-year profit — **$16.8M** against $14.2M and $9.6M.

The divergence lies in the **objective function**: the board maximises the three-year sum, where the best option is **B ($50.7M)**, while management maximises the first year, where the best is **C ($16.8M)**. Both optimisations are correct, and both parties are right **within their own problem**.

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who asks "which quantity are we maximising" reaches the right answer. An honest **Medium**, as planned.

**Separation from ST-009 and ST-026 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | The objective | What is wrong |
|---|---|---|
| **ST-009** S14(a) | the client names a **means** (subscription) | the objective is replaced by an instrument |
| **ST-026** S14(b) | the objective is right but **intermediate** (churn instead of profit) | optimising the intermediate contradicts the ultimate |
| **ST-047** S14(c) | the objective is right and ultimate **for each party** | the objectives **differ between parties**, and both optimisations are correct |

In (a) there is no objective. In (b) the objective is the wrong one. In (c) there are **two**, both genuine, and the conflict lies not in understanding but in the **incentive scheme**.

drills.title: The Point of No Return
drills.prompt: Wrenlocke is converting production to a new formulation. The project runs **24 months** and delivers **+$3.2M** cumulatively. The board asks: **do we launch?**

exhibit.title: Wrenlocke: conversion to the new formulation, $M
rows: **reversible**: the old line runs in parallel
**point of no return: the old line is dismantled**

key.decoy:
| Branch | Why it's reflexive | [DEC-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **"The result is positive — launch"** | a direct inference from the total | The total is correct: +$3.2M over 24 months. But mid-horizon there is an **irreversible step**, after which exit is impossible, and by that moment the accumulated loss is at its maximum. The branch calls for the headline figure and **will not show the trajectory** |
| **"Compare against alternative projects"** | the portfolio reflex | A comparison is appropriate but does not answer the question about the structure of risk **inside** this project |
key.cover:
| Branch | Synonyms (≥3) | [COV-JUST] |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The final result** | "what the project delivers" · "cumulative total" · "sum over the horizon" | Without the total the decision is unsupported |
| **2. The trajectory within the horizon** | "how it runs by month" · "the accumulation profile" · "where the minimum sits" | Without the trajectory the total conceals risk: an identical total can have very different profiles |
| **3. Points of irreversibility** | "where exit becomes impossible" · "after which step is there no way back" · "reversibility by stage" | **Load-bearing, and this is the muscle of the slot.** The project is reversible up to a certain step and irreversible after it. "Launch" is unsupported without answering **what we are risking at the point of no return** |
key.me:
| Pair | Status |
|---|---|
| 1 ↔ 2 | ⚠️ **conflation risk** — the total is the last point of the trajectory. Separated: branch 1 is the **value at the end**; branch 2 is the **profile in between** |
| 1 ↔ 3 | **independent** — magnitude vs reversibility |
| 2 ↔ 3 | ⚠️ **the slot's key pair** — the point of no return lies **on** the trajectory. Separated: branch 2 is **where the cumulative minimum sits**; branch 3 is **where the option to exit disappears**. Whether they coincide is the subject of the check |
| Positive total ↔ 1 | ⚠️ **duplicate** |
| Positive total ↔ 2, 3 | ⚠️ **opposition** — the total shows neither the profile nor the reversibility |
| Alternatives ↔ 1 | ⚠️ partial |
| Alternatives ↔ 2, 3 | **independent** |
key.drive:
| Branch | What to measure |
|---|---|
| 1. Total | cumulative result at month 24 |
| 2. Trajectory | cumulative total by month · month of the minimum · month it turns positive |
| 3. Irreversibility | the month after which exit is impossible · the accumulated loss at that moment · what is lost on exiting before and after |
key.order:
| Criterion | Legitimate start | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **irreversibility** | branch 3 | the cost of error is set by the point of no return, not by the total |
| size of effect | branch 2 | the profile shows the maximum loss |
| speed of verification | branch 1 | the total is already computed |
key.s0:
The total is correct and positive: **+$3.2M** over 24 months. The horizon is chosen correctly — 24 months, the whole project. The sequence of effects is accounted for: costs first, returns after.

The error is that **mid-horizon there is a point of no return**, and it falls exactly on the **maximum accumulated loss**: at month 8 the figure is **−$11.2M**, and from month 9 exit costs an additional **$19.4M** to repurchase the line.

The project therefore asks for agreement not to "+$3.2M over two years" but to "−$11.2M with the right to change our minds forfeited, in exchange for +$3.2M in 24 months."

**Top echelon: NO** — a candidate who checks reversibility stage by stage reaches the right answer. An honest **Medium**, as planned.

**Separation from ST-016 and ST-036 ([DUP], against BOTH):**

| | What is collapsed | What unfolding it reveals |
|---|---|---|
| **ST-016** S15(a) | the **horizon**: year 1 against three years | the sign changes over a longer horizon |
| **ST-036** S15(b) | the **sequence**: effects start in different months within the year | the sign changes inside the period |
| **ST-048** S15(c) | **reversibility**: horizon and sequence are both correct | mid-way the **right to exit disappears**, and this is visible in neither the total nor the profile alone |

In (a) look further. In (b) look inside. In (c) look at **rights** rather than money: the trajectory is known, and the question is when the option to stop disappears.
